title: The Year the World Caught Fire
teaser: Prague Spring. Paris barricades. Prague tanks. MLK and RFK assassinated. Tet Offensive. The old order fractures everywhere at once.

art.label: Art
art.content: Art ruptures in every direction. Conceptual Art crystallises with the formation of the Art & Language group and Lawrence Weiner's declaration that the idea alone is the work — execution optional. Land Art debuts with the "Earthworks" exhibition at Dwan Gallery, where Robert Smithson and Michael Heizer drag art out of galleries into desert landscapes. In June, Valerie Solanas shoots Andy Warhol; the Pope of Pop survives but withdraws from filmmaking. Meanwhile, 2001: A Space Odyssey premieres in April — Kubrick's monolith stands as enigmatic monument to an unknowable future. Art abandons objects for concepts, galleries for earth, screen for mind.

lit.label: Literature
lit.content: New Journalism captures the decade's acid-soaked energy: Tom Wolfe's The Electric Kool-Aid Acid Test follows Ken Kesey and the Merry Pranksters into psychedelic consciousness. Norman Mailer's The Armies of the Night makes the author both participant and chronicler of the Pentagon march. Philip K. Dick's Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep? asks what humanity means in a mechanised world. From the USSR, Solzhenitsyn's In the First Circle smuggles out witness to the Gulag. And García Márquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude, published the year before in Spanish, spreads like wildfire — magical realism offers escape and reflection alike.

phil.label: Philosophy
phil.content: Ideas arm the barricades. Marcuse's One-Dimensional Man (1964) becomes the bible of the New Left, diagnosing how consumer capitalism absorbs all opposition. Guy Debord's Society of the Spectacle (1967) provides the theoretical framework — modern life has become representation of life, image consuming reality. Slogans appear on Paris walls: "Sous les pavés, la plage!" — under the cobblestones, the beach. Meanwhile, Derrida delivers his lecture on différance in January, deconstruction quietly dismantling the certainties that the streets are loudly rejecting. Post-structuralism and street revolution share a target: inherited structures that pretend to be nature.

hist.label: History
hist.content: The year shatters the postwar consensus. In January, the Tet Offensive stuns America — victory in Vietnam, long promised, suddenly vanishes. In April, Martin Luther King Jr. is assassinated in Memphis; Robert Kennedy, announcing the news in Indianapolis, quotes Aeschylus on wisdom through suffering. Two months later, Kennedy himself is shot. In Prague, Dubček's "socialism with a human face" flowers in spring and dies in August under Soviet tanks. In Paris, students occupy the Sorbonne in May; ten million workers join them; de Gaulle nearly falls. In Chicago, police riot at the Democratic convention. In Mexico City, students are massacred days before the Olympics. Everywhere, youth confronts power — and power fights back.

conn.label: Connections
conn.content: What connects student barricades in Paris to tanks in Prague to assassinations in America? The same rupture: the postwar settlement cracking open. Debord's spectacle critique explains why television brought Tet's carnage into living rooms and broke the war consensus. Marcuse's "Great Refusal" gives vocabulary to Paris students and Berkeley activists alike. Derrida's deconstruction and the Situationists' détournement share a method — taking apart received structures to reveal what they conceal. Even 2001's enigmatic monolith mirrors the moment: something is coming that cannot be named, only experienced. The old authorities — state, party, church, academy — lose their spell simultaneously across cultures. Not coincidence: connection through circuits of books, films, radio, and the first global generation raised on television.

sources.title: Herbert Marcuse - Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy
Conceptual Art Movement - The Art Story

title: The Year the Eye Opened
teaser: Two books, published months apart, crack open the cosmos and the human body. Copernicus moves the Earth; Vesalius cuts into the flesh. Authority bows to observation.

art.label: Art
art.content: Mannerism reigns in Italy — elegant distortion over High Renaissance harmony. In Florence, Bronzino paints his icy portraits of the Medici, armoured dukes frozen in lacquered perfection. In Venice, Titian portrays Pope Paul III with psychological penetration that seems almost clinical. But the visual revolution of 1543 happens in woodcuts: Vesalius's Fabrica contains over 200 anatomical illustrations from Titian's workshop, dissected bodies posed like classical statues against Paduan landscapes. This is something new — art serving science, beauty illuminating truth. The écorché figures stand as if alive, muscles exposed, inviting the viewer to see what the text describes.

lit.label: Literature
lit.content: Scientific publishing comes of age. Copernicus's De revolutionibus arrives from Nuremberg in dense Latin prose, 405 pages of mathematical argument. Vesalius's Fabrica, from Basel, is a folio masterpiece where text and image interweave. Both books are dedicated to Pope Paul III — Copernicus seeking protection, Vesalius seeking patronage. In France, Rabelais has already published Gargantua and Pantagruel, savaging scholastic education and praising the humanist ideal of direct experience. The message is everywhere: read the book of nature, not just books about nature.

phil.label: Philosophy
phil.content: The epistemological revolution crystallises. For 1,300 years, Galen's anatomy textbooks were treated as scripture — read aloud during dissections while barber-surgeons cut. Vesalius changes the method: the physician must cut and look. He finds over 200 errors in Galen (who had dissected apes, not humans). Meanwhile, Copernicus displaces humanity from the cosmic centre. Both moves share a logic: observation trumps authority. This is proto-empiricism — the conviction that the eye can correct the book. Renaissance skepticism, recovered from Sextus Empiricus, provides the acid: how do we know what we claim to know? By looking.

hist.label: History
hist.content: Europe trembles between epochs. The Schmalkaldic League of Protestant princes challenges Emperor Charles V; the Council of Trent, delayed again, won't convene until 1545. Pope Paul III has just established the Roman Inquisition (1542) and will soon require licenses for printing. The year 1543 is a window — revolutionary books slip through before the Counter-Reformation tightens its grip. Copernicus dies in May, reportedly seeing his book only on his deathbed. Osiander, supervising publication, slips in an unauthorized preface calling heliocentrism a mere calculating device. The Church won't ban De revolutionibus until 1616 — but by then, the damage is done.

conn.label: Connections
conn.content: The two books share more than a publication year — they share a method. Vesalius corrects Galen by cutting bodies; Copernicus corrects Ptolemy by calculating orbits. Both replace the medieval deference to ancient texts with a new confidence: we can see for ourselves. The Fabrica's stunning woodcuts — possibly by Jan van Calcar of Titian's studio — make the invisible visible; Copernicus's diagrams do the same for celestial mechanics. Art and science fuse. The printing press multiplies both texts across Europe, creating a republic of letters where ideas travel faster than inquisitors. Galileo will read Copernicus; Harvey will read Vesalius. The Scientific Revolution begins not with one book but two — body and cosmos, opened in the same year.
conn.funFact: Vesalius personally stole corpses from gallows and graveyards for dissection. He once had to flee Leuven after an angry crowd discovered him retrieving a body from a public gibbet.

image.caption: Title page of Vesalius's De humani corporis fabrica, showing a crowded anatomy theater

title: Rome — The White Mantle
teaser: The millennium turns. Otto III dreams of a renewed Roman Empire from his palace on the Aventine. His former tutor rules as pope. Hungary receives its crown, Poland its archbishopric. And across Europe, the world puts on 'a white mantle of churches.'

art.label: Art
art.content: Ottonian illumination reaches its apex. At the monastery of Reichenau on Lake Constance, monks of the Liuthar Group produce the Gospels of Otto III — perhaps the most magnificent manuscript of the medieval West. Its double-page spreads show the emperor enthroned, receiving homage from personifications of Roma, Gallia, Germania, and Sclavinia. The style fuses Byzantine hieratic grandeur with Carolingian heritage: flattened figures, burnished gold backgrounds, brilliant mineral pigments. These books are theology made visible — Christ not as suffering man but as Pantocrator, lord of cosmos and time. UNESCO would later designate the Reichenau manuscripts as Memory of the World heritage.

lit.label: Literature
lit.content: The monk Raoul Glaber, writing a generation later, captures the mood: around the millennium, 'the whole world shook off the dust of ages and clothed itself in a white mantle of churches.' His Five Books of Histories becomes our window into millennial mentality — not mass panic, but fevered expectation. Gerbert of Aurillac, now Pope Sylvester II, leaves over 200 letters in elegant Latin, reading Virgil, Cicero, and Boethius when other churchmen barely read at all. His philosophical tract De rationali et de ratione uti revives classical logic. Literacy remains monastic, but the monasteries are stirring.

phil.label: Philosophy
phil.content: Gerbert represents a pre-Renaissance of rational method. Having studied in Muslim Spain at Vic and Ripoll, he reintroduces the abacus and Hindu-Arabic numerals to Latin Christendom, revolutionising calculation. He builds astronomical instruments — armillary spheres, celestial globes — that let students visualise Ptolemaic cosmology. His curriculum resurrects the seven liberal arts: the trivium (grammar, logic, rhetoric) preparing minds for the quadrivium (arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, music). Logic, dormant since Boethius, returns to European schools. Contemporaries whispered he'd learned sorcery from the Arabs; they could not believe mere study produced such knowledge.

hist.label: History
hist.content: Otto III, not yet twenty, rules from a palace on Rome's Aventine Hill, dreaming of Renovatio Imperii Romanorum — renewal of the Roman Empire. His pope is his former tutor, symbolically named Sylvester after Constantine's bishop. In March 1000, Otto journeys to Gniezno in Poland, praying at the tomb of martyred St. Adalbert; there he grants Poland an independent archbishopric, freeing it from German ecclesiastical control. At Christmas, Stephen I receives a crown from Sylvester, founding Christian Hungary. The Peace of God councils spread across France, bishops and abbots constraining feudal violence through oaths sworn on relics. Christendom is consolidating — and expanding.

conn.label: Connections
conn.content: The threads converge in Gerbert's dual role as pope and scholar. His curriculum at Reims and Bobbio trained a generation who would build the institutional Church. Otto III's vision required a learned pope who could articulate translatio imperii — the transfer of Roman authority. The illuminated gospels served this ideology: they showed emperor and evangelist occupying parallel heavens, sacred and secular power reflecting one another. When Otto gave Bolesław a replica of the Holy Lance at Gniezno, he was enacting manuscript theology in political ritual. The 'white mantle of churches' — Romanesque basilicas replacing Carolingian wood — would house the new learning that Gerbert helped revive.
conn.funFact: Gerbert's mastery of the abacus and astronomical instruments so astounded his contemporaries that after his death, legend claimed he'd made a pact with the devil and built a bronze head that answered questions about the future. The 'Scientist Pope' became a sorcerer in medieval folklore — proof that competence in mathematics looked like magic.

sources.title: Peace and Truce of God - Wikipedia

title: Constantinople — The End of an Empire
teaser: Two empires die. Two rise. An ancient faith dreams of reconciliation while gunpowder rewrites the rules of war. In Mainz, a printing press begins to transform how humanity thinks.

art.label: Art
art.content: As Constantinople falls, Late Byzantine art reaches its tragic culmination. The revered Hodegetria icon — believed painted by Saint Luke himself — is reportedly cut into four pieces during the sack. Yet Byzantine artistic traditions do not die: they scatter. Greek artists flee to Venetian Crete, founding the Cretan School that will produce El Greco a century later. Meanwhile, in Italy, Fra Angelico completes his silver chest paintings for Florence's Santissima Annunziata before returning to Rome. In the North, Rogier van der Weyden's workshop continues producing altarpieces that fuse Flemish realism with theological depth — a parallel revolution in rendering the sacred visible.
art.funFact: The Santissima Annunziata fresco was legendarily completed by an angel in 1252 — the painter fell asleep, and woke to find Mary's face finished by divine hand.

lit.label: Literature
lit.content: The fall generates immediate literary response. Manuel Doukas Chrysaphes, choralist to the last two emperors, composes a haunting threnos (lament) using Psalm 79: 'O God, the nations have invaded your inheritance.' Over a hundred Greek laments will follow, personifying Constantinople as a violated woman — vernacular poetry born from catastrophe. The Byzantine scholars fleeing West carry manuscripts that will fuel Italian humanism: Plato's dialogues, Aristotle's complete works, histories and rhetorics long unavailable in Latin Europe. Cardinal Bessarion, who escaped with his library, will bequeath 500 Greek manuscripts to Venice — the nucleus of the Biblioteca Marciana.
lit.funFact: Bessarion's 500-manuscript bequest to Venice was so vast it required a new building. The Biblioteca Marciana became Europe's first public library designed by an architect (Sansovino, 1537).

phil.label: Philosophy
phil.content: Nicholas of Cusa responds to the catastrophe with De Pace Fidei (On the Peace of Faith), a visionary dialogue proposing 'una religio in rituum varietate' — one religion in a variety of rites. Seventeen wise men representing all faiths gather in a heavenly council, seeking common ground through reason rather than crusade. It is the first sustained Western philosophical argument for religious tolerance, written in the immediate shadow of Christian defeat. Meanwhile, the Greek scholars arriving in Italy bring Neoplatonism directly from its source. The seeds planted now will bloom in Ficino's Florentine Academy, challenging scholastic Aristotelianism and reshaping Renaissance thought.
phil.funFact: Cusa wrote De Pace Fidei in just three weeks — a utopian dream composed at fever-pitch while Christendom panicked.

hist.label: History
hist.content: On May 29, after 53 days of siege, Mehmed II's Ottoman forces breach the Theodosian walls — defenses that had held for a thousand years — using massive bombards that herald a new age of gunpowder warfare. Constantine XI, last Roman Emperor, dies fighting in the breach. The city that Constantine the Great consecrated in 330 CE passes to Islam. Weeks later, on July 17, the Battle of Castillon ends the Hundred Years' War — the first major European battle won by field artillery. And in January, in Rome, the humanist Stefano Porcari was executed for plotting to overthrow Pope Nicholas V and restore the Roman Republic. Three empires reshaped in a single year.
hist.funFact: Mehmed moved 70 ships overland on greased logs to bypass the Byzantine chain across the Golden Horn — a 1.5-kilometer portage that stunned the defenders.

conn.label: Connections
conn.content: The threads converge in gunpowder and manuscripts. Mehmed's cannons demolish walls that had defined siegecraft for millennia, while Jean Bureau's artillery at Castillon shows that mounted knights are obsolete. Meanwhile, in Mainz, Gutenberg's printing press — producing Bibles by 1455 — will do to knowledge what cannons did to castles. The Byzantine scholars fleeing to Italy carry texts that will fuel both Renaissance humanism and the Reformation's return to Greek sources. Nicholas of Cusa's dream of religious dialogue through reason arrives precisely when such dialogue has become geopolitically urgent. Constantinople's fall doesn't end Byzantine civilization — it disperses it, seeding Europe with Greek learning at the exact moment when printing can amplify it.
conn.funFact: Gutenberg's first dated print isn't the Bible — it's a 1454 indulgence letter. The Church's fundraising tool preceded its Scripture.

sources.title: Greek scholars in the Renaissance - Wikipedia
